title: Overview
overview: JioOnePay is a **payment aggregation platform** built by Jio Platforms.
Merchants integrate with it once, instead of separately with every payment gateway or bank.
It sits directly **between the Merchant and the Payment Gateway/Bank** — the merchant only ever talks to JioOnePay, never to the bank directly.
It's a **microservices-based** system that routes, processes, and tracks payments across UPI, cards, wallets, and net banking.
why: A merchant that wants to accept UPI, cards, wallets, and net banking needs a separate integration for each one.
Every payment gateway has its own API, its own auth mechanism, and its own response format.
An aggregator absorbs all of that complexity once, so merchants integrate with a single, consistent API.
problem: Before aggregators, a merchant integrated **individually** with every gateway — high effort, high ongoing maintenance.
Adding a new payment method meant a brand-new integration project, not a config change.
There was no single place to monitor transactions, handle refunds, or retry failures across all payment methods.

intro: The architecture, stack, and my specific role in building this platform.

topics.heading: Application Architecture
points: **Microservices architecture** — each service is independently deployable, scalable, and easy to extend.
**API Gateway pattern** — `jop-gateway-service` is the single entry point for all traffic; it routes requests and handles resilience (circuit breaker, rate limiting).

topics.heading: Technologies Used
points: **Backend** — Core Java, Spring Boot, Spring Data JPA, Oracle Database, REST APIs.
**Frontend** — React, Next.js, TypeScript.

topics.heading: Key Features
points: Merchant Onboarding.
Payment Gateway Integration.
Multiple Payment Modes (UPI, Card, Wallet, Net Banking).
Dynamic Payment Routing.
Refunds & Transaction Monitoring.

topics.heading: My Roles & Responsibilities
points: Building REST APIs & business logic in Spring Boot, integrating new payment gateways, writing optimized Oracle SQL.
Frontend development in React/Next.js — building UI components and wiring them to backend APIs.
Code reviews, unit tests, debugging, and working with QA/Product/DevOps through release.

topics.heading: Challenges
points: **Problem** — integrating multiple payment gateways, each with different request/response formats and authentication mechanisms.
**Solution** — a common abstraction layer converts our internal request into the gateway-specific format, and transforms the gateway response back into our standard model.
**Impact** — merchants use a single API, and new gateways can be integrated without impacting existing merchants.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["Merchant"] --> B["JioOnePay"]
    B --> C["Payment Gateway"]
    C --> D["Bank"]
    D --> E["Response"]
architectureFlow.caption: The simplest possible view — JioOnePay sits between the merchant and the payment gateway/bank. The full flow is broken down layer by layer next.

simpleExample.scenario: A merchant's checkout page initiates a UPI payment through JioOnePay's REST API.
simpleExample.input: A merchant-initiated payment request for a fixed order amount, specifying UPI as the payment mode.
simpleExample.execution: JioOnePay validates the merchant and order, resolves UPI as the payment mode, and routes the request to the configured UPI-handling service/bank without the merchant needing to know which bank actually processes it.
simpleExample.output: A response containing a transactionId and a status (e.g. PENDING, moving to SUCCESS or FAILED once the bank responds).
simpleExample.explanation: The merchant only ever talks to one API — JioOnePay — regardless of which underlying bank or gateway actually settles the UPI transaction.

realWorldExample.scenario: Integrating multiple payment gateways, each with a different request/response format and authentication mechanism.
realWorldExample.input: An internal, standard payment request that must be sent to different gateways with completely different APIs.
realWorldExample.execution: Each gateway gets its own adapter implementing the same interface — converting the internal request into that gateway's specific format, and transforming its response back into JioOnePay's standard response model.
realWorldExample.output: Merchants and internal services always see the same standard request/response shape, regardless of which gateway actually handled the transaction.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is the Adapter design pattern applied at a system level — new gateways can be added by writing one new adapter class, without touching any existing merchant integration.

bestPractices: Build one common internal request/response model, and translate to/from each gateway's format only at the edges.
Keep gateway-specific quirks (auth, format, error codes) isolated inside that gateway's own adapter.
Centralize monitoring and refund handling at the aggregator layer for one consistent merchant experience.
commonMistakes: Letting one payment gateway's request/response shape leak into the shared internal domain model.
Hardcoding gateway-specific logic in shared services instead of isolating it behind an adapter.
Treating a new gateway integration as a special case instead of another implementation of the same adapter interface.
interviewTips: When asked "tell me about your project," lead with the business problem, then the solution.
Be ready to go deep on the Challenges section — interviewers often follow up on **how** you solved it, not just what you built.

faq.q: Why not let merchants integrate with each payment gateway directly?
faq.a: Every gateway has its own API, auth mechanism, and response format — pushing that complexity to every merchant multiplies the integration and maintenance cost across the whole ecosystem. An aggregator absorbs that complexity exactly once.

faq.q: What pattern solves the multi-gateway integration problem?
faq.a: An adapter layer: one standard internal request/response model, with a dedicated adapter class per gateway that converts to/from that gateway's specific format. Adding a gateway means adding one adapter, not touching existing code.

faq.q: What's the actual business impact of this abstraction?
faq.a: Merchants use a single, stable API regardless of how many gateways JioOnePay adds or changes behind the scenes — and new gateways can be onboarded without any risk to existing merchant integrations.

summary: JioOnePay is a payment aggregation platform: merchants integrate once instead of once per gateway.
A microservices architecture with an API Gateway (jop-gateway-service) as the single entry point routes and protects all traffic.
The core engineering challenge — many gateways, many formats — is solved with a per-gateway adapter layer around one standard internal model.
quickRevision: JioOnePay = payment aggregation platform by Jio Platforms.
Solves: merchants previously integrated individually with every gateway/bank.
Flow: customer picks payment mode → merchant calls JioOnePay REST API → routed to gateway/bank → response flows back.
Architecture: microservices + API Gateway pattern (jop-gateway-service, port 9985).
Backend stack: Core Java, Spring Boot, Spring Data JPA, Oracle DB, REST APIs.
Frontend stack: React, Next.js, TypeScript.
Key features: merchant onboarding, gateway integration, multiple payment modes, dynamic routing, refunds & monitoring.
Core challenge solved: a common abstraction layer normalizes every gateway's request/response format.

title: How a Request Reaches JioOnePay
overview: Before diving into JioOnePay's own services, it helps to see what happens **before** a request even reaches them — DNS resolution, load balancing, and HTTPS.
This is the same journey any web request takes, explained here using JioOnePay's real public domain, `payments.jio.com`, as the running example.
Understanding this layer explains why a customer's browser or a merchant's server never calls a Spring Boot service directly.
why: A customer's browser doesn't know or care which server, or which of many instances, actually runs the code — DNS and load balancing are what make that invisible.
Without this layer, scaling to multiple instances would require every client to somehow know about all of them.
problem: A raw IP address changes when infrastructure changes — a stable, memorable domain name is what a merchant integration actually depends on.
A single server handling every request directly would be a single point of failure and a scaling ceiling.
Without a reverse proxy/load balancer in front, adding or removing backend instances would require every client to know about the change.

intro: Five steps happen before any JioOnePay service code runs.

topics.heading: Domain & DNS Resolution
points: The customer's browser, or the merchant's server, sends a request to `payments.jio.com`.
DNS resolves that domain name to an IP address — the actual server(s) behind it can change without the domain ever changing.
This is what lets JioOnePay move or scale its infrastructure without every merchant integration needing to update anything.

topics.heading: HTTPS
points: The connection is encrypted end-to-end over HTTPS — essential for a payment platform, since requests carry merchant credentials and eventually payment details.
TLS termination — where the encrypted connection is actually decrypted — happens at the load balancer layer, before the request reaches any internal service.

topics.heading: Load Balancer (nginx)
points: JioOnePay uses **nginx** as its load balancer / reverse proxy in front of the API Gateway.
It spreads incoming requests across multiple instances of the next layer, and is what actually makes horizontal scaling possible.
Customers never call a Spring Boot service directly — nginx is the only thing the outside world can see.

topics.heading: Why Users Never Call a Spring Boot Service Directly
points: If clients called `jop-gateway-service` (or any backend) directly, scaling out would mean every client needs to know about every new instance.
A load balancer means backend instances can be added, removed, or replaced freely — clients only ever know about one stable entry point.
The load balancer, not each individual service, is the first line of defense — health checks here decide which backend instances even receive traffic.

topics.heading: Into the Gateway Layer
points: Once nginx picks a healthy backend instance, the request reaches `jop-gateway-service` — this is where the Architecture Layers page picks up, at Layer 1: Edge Layer.

topics.heading: The Full Request Journey (Sequence)
points: The same journey shown as a sequence over time, instead of a static flow.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram

    participant DNS as DNS
    participant LB as nginx Load Balancer
    participant GW as jop-gateway-service

architectureFlow.caption: Everything before the gateway — resolving a domain name to a specific, load-balanced backend instance.

stepByStepFlow: Customer or merchant sends a request to payments.jio.com.
DNS resolves the domain to an IP address for JioOnePay's infrastructure.
The TLS/HTTPS handshake completes, encrypting the connection.
nginx (Load Balancer) picks a healthy backend instance to route the request to.
The request reaches jop-gateway-service, and the Architecture Layers' Edge Layer takes over from here.

simpleExample.scenario: A merchant's server calls JioOnePay to initiate a payment.
simpleExample.code:
GET https://payments.jio.com/jop/initiate/payment/request

1. DNS resolves payments.jio.com
2. TLS handshake completes
3. nginx picks a healthy jop-gateway-service instance
4. Request forwarded to jop-gateway-service
simpleExample.input: An HTTPS request to JioOnePay's public domain.
simpleExample.execution: The request passes through DNS resolution, TLS termination, and load balancing before any JioOnePay-specific code runs.
simpleExample.output: The request lands on a specific, healthy jop-gateway-service instance, ready to be routed by the Edge Layer.
simpleExample.explanation: None of this is JioOnePay-specific logic — it's the same journey any web request takes before reaching an application, using JioOnePay's real domain as the example.

realWorldExample.scenario: One jop-gateway-service instance fails its health check during a deploy.
realWorldExample.code:
1. nginx periodically health-checks each backend instance
2. Instance X fails its check
3. nginx stops routing new requests to Instance X
4. Existing requests to Instance X may still complete or fail
5. Once Instance X passes health checks again, nginx resumes routing to it
realWorldExample.input: A backend instance becoming temporarily unhealthy.
realWorldExample.execution: nginx's health checking removes the bad instance from rotation automatically, without any client noticing beyond perhaps one failed request during the transition.
realWorldExample.output: Customers continue to reach a healthy instance; the failing one is quietly excluded until it recovers.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why customers never call a Spring Boot service directly — the load balancer is what absorbs individual instance failures.

comparisonTables.title: Without vs With a Load Balancer
rows: Clients must know about every new instance
New instances just join the pool
Traffic quietly shifts to healthy instances

advantages: Customers and merchants only ever depend on one stable domain, regardless of how the backend infrastructure changes underneath it.
Instance-level failures are absorbed automatically, before they ever reach the customer.
tradeoffs: Terminating TLS at the load balancer centralizes certificate management, at the cost of the connection being unencrypted for the short hop between the load balancer and the backend (typically mitigated by keeping that hop within a trusted private network).
bestPractices: Never expose individual backend instances directly — always route through a load balancer.
Terminate TLS at the load balancer so certificate management is centralized, not duplicated per service.
commonMistakes: Assuming DNS resolution and load balancing are irrelevant to application-level design — they directly shape how failures and scaling actually behave.
Hardcoding a specific backend instance's address anywhere in a client integration.
interviewTips: If asked to design any web-facing system, mention DNS and load balancing before jumping to application architecture — it shows you think about the full request path.
Be ready to explain what a health check protects against at this layer, versus what Circuit Breaker protects against deeper in the system.

faq.q: Why does JioOnePay use nginx specifically, and not a cloud load balancer?
faq.a: nginx acts as the reverse proxy/load balancer in front of the gateway; the exact choice of nginx vs. a managed cloud load balancer is an infrastructure decision — either serves the same purpose of spreading traffic across healthy instances and hiding backend topology from clients.

faq.q: Does DNS resolution happen on every single request?
faq.a: No — DNS responses are cached (with a TTL) by the client's OS/browser/resolver, so repeated requests from the same client typically reuse a cached IP instead of resolving fresh every time.

faq.q: What's the difference between this Load Balancer and the resilience checks inside jop-gateway-service?
faq.a: The Load Balancer decides which backend instance receives a request at all, based on instance health. Once a request reaches jop-gateway-service, a separate layer (Circuit Breaker, Rate Limiter, Bulkhead) protects against a specific downstream service failing — they operate at different layers, for different failure types.

summary: Before any JioOnePay-specific logic runs, a request resolves payments.jio.com via DNS, completes a TLS handshake, and is routed by nginx to a healthy backend instance.
This layer is what makes scaling and failure recovery invisible to the customer — they only ever see one stable domain.
From here, the request enters jop-gateway-service, where the Architecture Layers page's Edge Layer takes over.
quickRevision: JioOnePay's public domain is payments.jio.com.
DNS resolves the domain to an IP; responses are cached with a TTL, not resolved on every request.
TLS/HTTPS terminates at the load balancer layer, before any backend service sees the request.
nginx is the load balancer/reverse proxy in front of jop-gateway-service.
Load balancer health checks decide which backend instances receive traffic at all — a separate concern from Circuit Breaker/Rate Limiter/Bulkhead deeper in the Edge Layer.
Customers/merchants never call a Spring Boot service directly — only ever the load balancer's stable domain.

title: Complete Payment Lifecycle
overview: Before looking at individual services, it helps to see the **entire journey** a payment takes — from the merchant's first request to the merchant getting notified of the outcome.
This page walks through that full journey once, end-to-end, using JioOnePay's actual service names.
Every layer and service introduced later in this section is just a closer look at one step in this same journey.
why: Jumping straight into ~15 named microservices without first seeing the journey they're part of makes each one feel arbitrary.
Seeing the whole lifecycle once gives every later detail — a specific service, a specific layer — an obvious place to slot into.
problem: A payment isn't one request-response — it's a multi-step journey involving a redirect, a UI, a mode choice, and two independent completion signals.
Without seeing the full journey first, it's easy to assume a payment is confirmed the moment the customer's browser redirects back — which is not actually true here.

intro: The journey has three natural parts: getting the customer to the checkout page, processing their chosen payment mode, and confirming the outcome to everyone who needs to know.

topics.heading: Part 1 — Initiation to Checkout
points: The Merchant sends an initiate request; `jop-payment-link-service` validates it and stores the context in Redis under a token.
It returns an auto-submitting HTML form back to the merchant's page.
The merchant's browser auto-submits that form, which routes through the gateway to `jop-payment-options-service`.

The Options service looks up the token, fetches the merchant's configured payment modes, and redirects to the actual checkout UI — which calls back to the Options service the moment it renders, to load the dashboard.

topics.heading: Part 2 — Mode Selection to Gateway
points: The customer picks a mode (UPI, Card, Wallet, Net Banking) from the options shown.
That choice routes to a mode-specific service — `jop-upi-service`, `jop-ccdcnb-service`, or a wallet/other service.
Non-card modes call a `jop-core-*-service` adapter, which talks to the actual bank/gateway. Card payments instead go to the isolated `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, which calls the bank/ACS directly.

topics.heading: Part 3 — Completion & Notification
points: The bank/gateway sends back **two independent signals**: a browser redirect (to `jop-payment-b2b-service`) and a server-to-server callback (to `jop-payment-s2s-service`).
`jop-payment-s2s-service` is the authoritative one — it writes the final outcome to the Oracle transaction database and sends the merchant a webhook with a checksum.
`jop-payment-b2b-service` redirects the customer's browser back to the merchant, and typically also triggers an internal webhook to `jop-payment-s2s-service` to keep both paths in sync.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Merchant
    participant GW as jop-gateway-service
    participant PLS as jop-payment-link-service
    participant PGD as jop-core-pg-details-service
    participant R as Redis
    participant CHK as jop-payment-options-service
    participant MODE as Mode Service (UPI / CCDCNB / Wallet)
    participant CORE as jop-core-* / PCI service
    participant BANK as Bank / PG / ACS
    participant B2B as jop-payment-b2b-service
    participant S2S as jop-payment-s2s-service
    participant DB as Oracle Txn DB

    M->>GW: 1. Initiate payment request
    GW->>PLS: forward
    PLS->>PGD: validate checksum + config
    PLS->>R: store token to jopMsg
    PLS-->>M: htmlForm (Base64)
    M->>GW: 2. Browser auto-POST
    GW->>CHK: forward
    CHK->>R: lookup token
    CHK->>PGD: fetch configured modes
    CHK-->>M: redirect to Payment Options UI
    M->>CHK: 3. UI calls back on render
    M->>MODE: 4. Customer selects mode
    MODE->>CORE: forward (or direct to PCI service for cards)
    CORE->>BANK: 5. Process payment
    BANK-->>B2B: 6a. Browser return
    BANK-->>S2S: 6b. Server callback
    S2S->>DB: 7. Write transaction outcome
    B2B-->>M: 8a. Redirect customer
    S2S-->>M: 8b. Webhook plus checksum
architectureFlow.caption: The complete journey, using JioOnePay's real service names, from initiation through both completion paths.

simpleExample.scenario: A customer completes a ₹499 UPI payment, followed end-to-end.
simpleExample.code:
1. Merchant -> jop-payment-link-service: initiate
2. jop-payment-link-service -> Redis: store token
3. Merchant browser auto-POSTs -> jop-payment-options-service
4. Customer selects UPI on the checkout UI
5. jop-upi-service -> jop-core-<provider>-service -> Bank
6. Bank -> jop-payment-s2s-service (server callback)
7. jop-payment-s2s-service -> Oracle DB + merchant webhook
simpleExample.input: A single UPI payment request for a fixed amount.
simpleExample.execution: The request moves through initiation, checkout, mode selection, and gateway processing, exactly once, in order.
simpleExample.output: The merchant receives a webhook confirming SUCCESS; the customer sees a confirmation screen via the browser-redirect path.
simpleExample.explanation: Every payment mode follows this same overall shape — only the mode-selection and gateway-integration steps differ based on what the customer picked.

realWorldExample.scenario: The bank's server callback (S2S) arrives, but the customer's browser never redirects back — they closed the tab.
realWorldExample.code:
BANK -->> S2S: server callback (SUCCESS)
S2S ->> DB: write SUCCESS
S2S -->> Merchant: webhook + checksum

# Browser redirect (B2B) never happens - customer closed the tab
# Merchant still gets the correct outcome via the webhook
realWorldExample.input: A completed payment where the customer's browser never returns to the merchant's site.
realWorldExample.execution: The server-to-server callback still fires independently of the browser, since it's a direct bank-to-JioOnePay signal, not something that depends on the customer's browser at all.
realWorldExample.output: The merchant is correctly notified of the SUCCESS outcome via the webhook, even though the browser-redirect path never completed.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why the server callback (S2S), not the browser redirect (B2B), is the authoritative signal — the browser path can fail for reasons that have nothing to do with whether the payment actually succeeded.

comparisonTables.title: Browser Redirect (B2B) vs Server Callback (S2S)
rows: Get the customer back to the merchant's site
Yes — tab closed, network drop, etc.
Retried by the bank if delivery fails
The database write and the merchant webhook

advantages: Splitting the journey into initiation, mode selection, and completion makes each part independently understandable.
Having two independent completion signals means the transaction outcome doesn't depend on the customer's browser behaving well.
tradeoffs: Two independent completion paths (B2B and S2S) mean extra reconciliation logic is needed to keep the customer-facing and merchant-facing outcomes in sync.
bestPractices: Always treat the server-to-server callback as the source of truth — never the browser redirect alone.
Store enough context in Redis at initiation time that every later step (checkout, mode selection) can be resumed from just the token.
commonMistakes: Assuming a customer reaching the browser-redirect page means the payment succeeded — it only means the browser came back, not that the transaction did.
Treating the Payment Options UI's render-time call as optional — it's how the UI actually gets the merchant's configured modes.
interviewTips: When asked to describe your project, walk through this exact lifecycle in order — it demonstrates you understand the flow, not just a list of service names.
Be ready to explain, specifically, why the browser redirect isn't trusted as the confirmation of success.

faq.q: What happens if the browser redirect (B2B) never fires?
faq.a: The transaction is still correctly recorded and the merchant is still correctly notified, because the server callback (S2S) to jop-payment-s2s-service is independent of the browser — it's a direct signal from the bank.

faq.q: Why does the UI call back to jop-payment-options-service the moment it renders?
faq.a: That's how it fetches the actual data to display — the merchant's configured payment modes, fetched live rather than baked into the redirect itself.

faq.q: Is the token stored in Redis at initiation reused throughout the whole journey?
faq.a: Yes — it's what lets the Checkout Layer, and later steps, look up the original request's context (the jopMsg) without the merchant needing to resend it.

summary: A JioOnePay payment is a multi-step journey: initiation and checksum validation, a checkout redirect, mode selection, gateway processing, and two independent completion signals.
The server-to-server callback, not the browser redirect, is the authoritative confirmation of a payment's outcome.
Every service and layer covered next in this section is a closer look at one specific step in this same journey.
quickRevision: Initiation: jop-payment-link-service validates checksum/config, stores context in Redis under a token.
Checkout: merchant's browser auto-POSTs the returned HTML form; jop-payment-options-service serves the payment options UI.
The UI calls jop-payment-options-service on render to load its actual data (configured modes).
Mode selection routes to jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service / wallet, or to the isolated PCI service for cards.
The bank sends TWO signals: a browser redirect (jop-payment-b2b-service) and a server callback (jop-payment-s2s-service).
jop-payment-s2s-service is authoritative — it writes to the Oracle DB and sends the merchant's webhook.
jop-payment-b2b-service only handles the customer-facing redirect, and often also triggers an internal webhook to keep S2S in sync.

title: Architecture Layers
overview: JioOnePay's payment flow is organized into **seven layers**, each with one clear responsibility — from receiving the merchant's request to notifying them of the final outcome.
Understanding the system layer by layer, instead of all ~15+ payment-related microservices at once, is the only way the architecture actually makes sense.
Each layer maps to real running services — this isn't a generic textbook diagram, it's how JioOnePay is actually built.
why: A payment aggregator genuinely has this many moving parts — explaining them as one flat list of services hides *why* each one exists.

Layers group services by responsibility, so a new engineer, or an interviewer, can reason about one concern at a time: routing, checkout, mode selection, card security, gateway integration, completion.
problem: Without layering, ~15+ payment services look like an undifferentiated mess — impossible to reason about or explain clearly in an interview.
Card payments specifically need to be isolated for PCI-DSS compliance — mixing that into a generic "payment mode" layer would be a real security and audit problem.

intro: The seven layers, in the order a request actually passes through them.

topics.heading: Layer 1 — Edge Layer
points: **Purpose** — the single public entry point for all merchant traffic; nothing about payments is decided here, no business logic, no database.
**Services** — Load Balancer, `jop-gateway-service` (:9985) — Spring Boot 3.1.5, Java 17, Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux + Netty, reactive, non-blocking).

**Routing** — all routes live in one Zookeeper key, `config-properties` (Base64 JSON); `ApiRoutesLocatorService` builds the in-memory route table from it and `ZookeeperRefreshListener` auto-refreshes on change — no restart needed, and no Consul/Kubernetes discovery involved.
**Resilience order** — every request is checked by **Circuit Breaker**, then **Rate Limiter**, then **Bulkhead**, in that exact order, before being forwarded.

**Failure handling** — Circuit Breaker failures redirect to `/fallback` (only on HTTP 5xx); Rate Limiter and Bulkhead reject immediately with their own error instead. All three are tunable **per route** via a second Zookeeper key, `resilience-properties`.
topics.mermaidCaption: The exact order resilience checks run in, for every request the gateway receives.

topics.heading: Layer 2 — Payment Initiation Layer
points: **Purpose** — receives the merchant's initiate request, validates it, and hands back a checkout form.
**Services** — `jop-payment-link-service` (`POST /jop/initiate/payment/request`), `jop-core-pg-details-service` (merchant config/modes), Redis (token → jopMsg).

**Request Flow** — the merchant calls `jop-payment-link-service`, which validates a checksum and merchant config against `jop-core-pg-details-service`, stores the request context in Redis under a token, and returns an auto-submitting HTML form (Base64-encoded) back to the merchant.
**Failure handling** — an invalid checksum or unknown merchant config is rejected here, before any token is even created.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Merchant: initiate request"] --> B["jop-payment-link-service"]
    B --> C["jop-core-pg-details-service: validate checksum + config"]
    B --> D[("Redis: token to jopMsg")]
    B --> E["Return htmlForm (Base64) to Merchant"]
topics.mermaidCaption: The request is validated and stashed under a token before any UI is even shown.

topics.heading: Layer 3 — Checkout Layer
points: **Purpose** — renders the payment options page the customer actually sees and interacts with.
**Services** — `jop-payment-options-service` (`/payment-options` → redirect UI), the Payment Options UI itself.

**Request Flow** — the merchant's page auto-POSTs the htmlForm from Layer 2; `jop-gateway-service` forwards it to `jop-payment-options-service`, which looks up the token in Redis, fetches the merchant's dynamically configured modes, and redirects the browser to the UI. The UI itself calls back to the options service on render, to load the dashboard.
**Failure handling** — an expired or unknown token at this point means the checkout session itself is invalid, and the customer sees an error instead of a payment form.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Merchant Browser
    participant GW as jop-gateway-service
    participant CHK as jop-payment-options-service
    participant R as Redis
    participant UI as Payment Options UI
    M->>GW: auto-POST htmlForm
    GW->>CHK: forward
    CHK->>R: lookup token
    CHK-->>UI: redirect
    UI->>CHK: payment-dashboard request on render
topics.mermaidCaption: The UI calls back to the options service the moment it renders — that's how it gets the actual payment modes to display.

topics.heading: Layer 4 — Payment Mode Orchestration Layer
points: **Purpose** — routes the customer's chosen payment mode (UPI, Card, Net Banking, Wallet, and more) to the service that handles it.
**Services** — `jop-upi-service`, `jop-ccdcnb-service` (credit/debit card + net banking), plus wallet and other mode-specific services.
**Dynamic configuration** — payment modes aren't hardcoded; they're configured per-merchant in an admin portal, and the Checkout Layer only shows modes actually enabled for that merchant.

**Request Flow** — the UI calls the mode-specific service directly (via the gateway) once the customer picks a mode; card payments are handed off to the PCI Layer instead of following the standard path.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Customer selects mode"] --> B{"Which mode?"}
    B -->|"UPI"| C["jop-upi-service"]
    B -->|"Card / Net Banking"| D["jop-ccdcnb-service"]
    B -->|"Wallet / other"| E["Wallet service"]
    D -->|"Card specifically"| F["PCI Layer"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Mode selection branches here; card payments are handed off to the isolated PCI layer.

topics.heading: Layer 5 — PCI Layer
points: **Purpose** — the only layer allowed to touch raw card data (PAN), isolated so the rest of the system never has to be PCI-DSS scoped.
**Services** — `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, reached specifically for credit/debit card payments.

**Why it's isolated** — PCI-DSS compliance requires strict controls (encryption, access logging, network segmentation) around anything that touches a card number; keeping that inside one dedicated service means the other services never need those controls.

**Request Flow** — `jop-ccdcnb-service` hands card payments to `jop-pci-ccdc-card-service`, which calls the bank/ACS **directly** over HTTP — it bypasses the normal core-gateway-adapter path entirely, keeping the card-data path as short as possible.
topics.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["jop-ccdcnb-service"] -->|"CC/DC only"| B["jop-pci-ccdc-card-service"]
    B -->|"direct HTTP"| C["Bank / ACS"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Card data takes the shortest, most isolated path in the entire system — straight to the bank.

topics.heading: Layer 6 — Gateway Integration Layer
points: **Purpose** — talks to the actual external payment gateways and banks on behalf of every non-card payment mode.

**Services** — `jop-core-*-service` adapters, one per provider: Razorpay, PhonePe, Easebuzz, Citi, and others — each with its own **Apache HttpClient 5 wrapper**; there's no shared HTTP client abstraction across providers, since different banks have genuinely different requirements.
**Routing** — Zookeeper-based dynamic routing picks a provider by health, cost, or merchant preference.

**Security** — bank-grade integrations use **mTLS** — e.g. Citi loads a **PKCS12 client certificate** over **TLS 1.2**, so the bank verifies JioOnePay's identity too, not just the reverse. Timeouts, proxy settings, and certificate paths come from Zookeeper config, tunable per environment without a redeploy.

**Internal calls (how this layer is reached)** — services call each other via **Spring Cloud OpenFeign** (`@FeignClient(name = "service-name")`), resolved at call-time through Zookeeper-based service discovery — a plain Java interface that reads like a local method call. These calls go directly service-to-service and never pass through the Edge Layer's gateway, which is for external client traffic only.

**No message broker** — every call, internal or external, is a direct synchronous HTTP request/response; there's no Kafka or RabbitMQ anywhere in this system. `jop-common-redis-lib` provides shared caching (idempotency keys, session state) across services — that's caching, not messaging.

**Failure handling** — Spring Retry (`@Retryable`) layers on top of some Feign clients for transient internal failures; external retries are handled per-adapter. This synchronous, broker-less design is exactly why the Edge Layer's Circuit Breaker/Bulkhead exist — to contain a slow downstream call, since there's no async buffer to fall back on.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service"] --> B["jop-core-razorpay-service"]
    A --> C["jop-core-phonepe-service"]
    A --> D["jop-core-citi-service"]
    B --> E["External PG / Bank"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
topics.mermaidCaption: One adapter per provider — new gateways are added here without touching upstream services.

topics.heading: Layer 7 — Completion Layer
points: **Purpose** — records the final outcome and tells everyone who needs to know: the customer's browser and the merchant's system.
**Services** — `jop-payment-b2b-service` (browser redirect back to the merchant) and `jop-payment-s2s-service` (server-to-server callback, database write, merchant webhook).

**Two separate completion paths** — the bank returns the customer's **browser** to `jop-payment-b2b-service` for the redirect, while a **server-to-server callback** goes to `jop-payment-s2s-service` independently; the two aren't the same request.

**Request Flow** — `jop-payment-s2s-service` writes the outcome to the Oracle transaction database and sends the merchant a webhook with a checksum; `jop-payment-b2b-service` redirects the customer's browser back to the merchant, and often also triggers an internal webhook to `jop-payment-s2s-service` to keep both paths in sync.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Bank / PG"] -->|"browser return"| B["jop-payment-b2b-service"]
    A -->|"server callback"| C["jop-payment-s2s-service"]
    B -.->|"internal webhook"| C
    C --> D[("Oracle Txn DB")]
    B --> E["Redirect customer to Merchant"]
    C --> F["Webhook + checksum to Merchant"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Two independent completion paths that converge on the same transaction record.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    L1["Layer 1: Edge"] --> L2["Layer 2: Payment Initiation"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Checkout"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4: Payment Mode Orchestration"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: PCI"]
    L4 --> L6["Layer 6: Gateway Integration"]
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7: Completion"]
architectureFlow.caption: The seven layers in request order — each detailed individually above.

simpleExample.scenario: Tracing a single ₹499 UPI payment through all seven layers.
simpleExample.code:
1. Merchant -> Edge Layer (jop-gateway-service)
2. Edge -> Payment Initiation (jop-payment-link-service): checksum OK, token stored
3. Checkout Layer (jop-payment-options-service): renders UPI as an available mode
4. Payment Mode Orchestration: customer selects UPI -> jop-upi-service
5. Gateway Integration: jop-upi-service -> jop-core-<provider>-service -> Bank
6. Completion: server callback -> jop-payment-s2s-service -> DB + merchant webhook
simpleExample.input: A single UPI payment request for ₹499.
simpleExample.execution: The request passes through all seven layers exactly once, in order, each one adding exactly one piece of the puzzle.
simpleExample.output: The merchant receives a webhook confirming SUCCESS, and the customer sees a confirmation screen.
simpleExample.explanation: This is the same shape every payment mode follows — only Layer 4 and 5/6 differ based on which mode the customer picked.

realWorldExample.scenario: A card payment specifically, which routes through the isolated PCI Layer instead of the standard Gateway Integration path.
realWorldExample.code:
1-3. Same as UPI: Edge -> Payment Initiation -> Checkout
4. Payment Mode Orchestration: customer selects Card -> jop-ccdcnb-service
5. PCI Layer: jop-ccdcnb-service hands off to jop-pci-ccdc-card-service
6. jop-pci-ccdc-card-service calls the Bank/ACS DIRECTLY (bypasses jop-core-*-service adapters)
7. Completion: same as any other mode
realWorldExample.input: A card payment, where raw card data must be handled.
realWorldExample.execution: Layer 4 detects the mode is a card payment and routes to the PCI Layer instead of a standard core-gateway adapter; the PCI service talks to the bank directly, keeping the card-data path as short and isolated as possible.
realWorldExample.output: The transaction completes through a completely separate path for the card-handling step, converging back to the same Completion Layer as every other mode.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is exactly why the PCI Layer exists as its own thing — it's the one place in the whole system where card data flows, and it's designed to be as small and auditable as possible.

rows: Forwarded to mode service (or PCI Layer for cards)
Per-adapter retries, mTLS for bank-grade calls

comparisonTables.title: Circuit Breaker vs Rate Limiter vs Bulkhead (Layer 1: Edge)
Internal (Feign) vs External (Apache HttpClient + mTLS) — Layer 6

Goes through the Edge Layer gateway?
No — direct from the owning microservice

advantages: Layering means a new engineer, or an interviewer, can reason about one responsibility at a time instead of ~15+ services at once.
Isolating card data into one PCI-scoped service keeps the other services entirely out of PCI-DSS audit scope.
tradeoffs: More layers means more network hops for a single payment — each layer boundary is a potential point of latency or failure.
Two independent completion paths (B2B and S2S) require careful reconciliation logic to keep them in sync, rather than one simpler path.
bestPractices: Isolate card data (PCI Layer) completely from every other payment mode — never let PAN flow through a shared/generic service.
Keep mode selection dynamic (admin-configured) rather than hardcoded, so enabling a new mode for a merchant is a config change, not a deployment.
Give the two completion paths (browser redirect vs server callback) independent responsibility — never assume one guarantees the other happened.
commonMistakes: Assuming the browser redirect (B2B) alone confirms a successful payment — only the server-to-server callback (S2S) is authoritative.
Letting card-handling logic leak outside the PCI Layer, expanding PCI-DSS audit scope unnecessarily.
Treating all payment services as one flat list instead of reasoning about them layer by layer.
interviewTips: When asked to explain this architecture, walk through the layers in request order — it's a much stronger answer than listing services alphabetically.
Be ready to explain specifically why card payments get their own isolated layer — this is a favorite senior-level follow-up.

faq.q: Why does the browser redirect and the server callback go to two different services?
faq.a: The browser redirect (B2B) is for the customer's experience — getting them back to the merchant's site. The server callback (S2S) is the authoritative, machine-to-machine confirmation that's actually trusted for recording the transaction and notifying the merchant. Relying on the browser redirect alone would be unreliable — a customer can close the tab before it completes.

faq.q: Why is there a separate PCI Layer instead of handling cards inside the normal Gateway Integration Layer?
faq.a: PCI-DSS compliance requires strict controls around anything that touches raw card data. Isolating that into one dedicated service means only that one service needs the heavy compliance controls — everything else in the system stays out of PCI scope entirely.

faq.q: How does the Checkout Layer know which payment modes to show a specific merchant?
faq.a: It's not hardcoded — modes are configured per-merchant in an admin portal, and jop-payment-options-service fetches that configuration live from jop-core-pg-details-service before rendering the checkout page.

faq.q: Why is the Edge Layer gateway built on Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux) instead of a traditional servlet-based gateway?
faq.a: Spring Cloud Gateway is reactive and non-blocking (built on WebFlux + Netty), which lets a single gateway instance handle many concurrent in-flight requests — including slow downstream calls — without needing a thread per request.

faq.q: Why use Feign instead of a raw RestTemplate or WebClient for internal calls in Layer 6?
faq.a: Feign lets you declare a REST client as a plain Java interface — you call it like a local method, and Feign plus Zookeeper-based discovery handle resolving the real address and making the HTTP call, cutting out repetitive client boilerplate.

summary: JioOnePay's payment flow is organized into seven layers: Edge, Payment Initiation, Checkout, Payment Mode Orchestration, PCI, Gateway Integration, and Completion.
Each layer has one clear responsibility, and card payments specifically get isolated into their own PCI layer for compliance.
The Completion layer has two independent paths — browser redirect and server callback — with the server callback being the authoritative source of truth.
quickRevision: 7 layers in request order: Edge → Payment Initiation → Checkout → Payment Mode Orchestration → (PCI | Gateway Integration) → Completion.
Edge: jop-gateway-service, port 9985, routes + protects.
Payment Initiation: jop-payment-link-service validates checksum/config, stores token in Redis, returns an HTML auto-submit form.
Checkout: jop-payment-options-service serves the payment options UI, fetching dynamically configured modes.
Payment Mode Orchestration: routes to jop-upi-service / jop-ccdcnb-service / wallet, based on admin-configured modes per merchant.
PCI Layer: jop-pci-ccdc-card-service is the only service allowed to touch card PAN, calling the bank/ACS directly.
Gateway Integration: one jop-core-*-service adapter per provider (Razorpay, PhonePe, Easebuzz, Citi), each with its own HttpClient wrapper — no shared abstraction across providers.
Completion: jop-payment-b2b-service (browser redirect) and jop-payment-s2s-service (server callback, DB write, merchant webhook) are two independent paths.
No message broker anywhere — every call, internal (Feign + Zookeeper discovery) or external (per-adapter HttpClient + mTLS), is direct synchronous HTTP.
jop-common-redis-lib = shared caching (idempotency keys, session state), not messaging. jop-common-lib = shared Feign/Zookeeper/Retry setup.

Service Details — the individual services inside these layers

title: Service Details
overview: Now that the layers are clear, this page looks at the **individual services** inside them — what each one owns, and how they depend on each other.
Rather than long explanations, each service is documented as a compact table: purpose, dependencies, data, and failure behavior.
This is the level of detail an interviewer's follow-up question — "what does that specific service actually do?" — is really asking about.
why: Layers explain the *shape* of the system; services explain what's actually running inside each box.
A senior-level interview often drills into one specific service — being able to describe its exact responsibility, not just its layer, is what separates a strong answer.
problem: Without documenting services individually, "what does jop-payment-link-service do" only has a layer-level answer, not a precise one.
A shared understanding of who owns which data — which service, which table — is what prevents two services from writing inconsistent state to the same data.

intro: Data ownership first, since it shapes everything else, then each group of services.

topics.heading: How Transaction Data Is Modeled
points: A shared **transactions table** holds one row per transaction per merchant, keyed by a unique **ORN** (order reference number), with a status column tracking the transaction's lifecycle.

A separate **amount table** holds one or more rows per transaction — this is what allows a single transaction to be paid using **combined payment modes** (e.g. part TWID, part UPI) instead of forcing one mode per transaction.
This split exists specifically so a transaction's identity (the ORN + status) stays simple and singular, while the amount breakdown can flex to however many modes actually contributed to it.

topics.heading: Edge & Initiation Services
points: **jop-gateway-service** — the Edge Layer's single entry point; routes, protects, no business logic.
**jop-payment-link-service** — validates the initiate request's checksum and merchant config, stores context in Redis under a token, returns the checkout HTML form.
**jop-core-pg-details-service** — owns merchant configuration and enabled payment modes; queried by both the Initiation and Checkout layers.

topics.heading: Checkout & Mode Services
points: **jop-payment-options-service** — serves the payment options UI, looks up the Redis token, fetches configured modes.
**jop-upi-service** — handles the UPI payment mode.
**jop-ccdcnb-service** — handles Card and Net Banking modes; hands card payments off to the PCI service.
**jop-pci-ccdc-card-service** — the only service allowed to touch raw card data; calls the bank/ACS directly.

topics.heading: Gateway Integration Services
points: **jop-core-razorpay-service, jop-core-phonepe-service, jop-core-citi-service, jop-core-easebuzz-service**, and similar — one adapter per external provider, each with its own Apache HttpClient 5 wrapper.
Each adapter reads/writes against the shared transaction and amount tables for its own transactions, keyed by ORN.

topics.heading: Completion Services
points: **jop-payment-b2b-service** — handles the customer's browser redirect back to the merchant.
**jop-payment-s2s-service** — the authoritative server-to-server callback handler; writes the final status to the transactions table and sends the merchant's webhook.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    LINK["jop-payment-link-service"] --> PGD["jop-core-pg-details-service"]
    CHK["jop-payment-options-service"] --> PGD
    CHK --> R[("Redis")]
    UPI["jop-upi-service"] --> CORE["jop-core-*-service adapters"]
    CCDCNB["jop-ccdcnb-service"] --> PCI["jop-pci-ccdc-card-service"]
    CCDCNB --> CORE
    CORE --> DB[("Transactions + Amount tables")]
    S2S["jop-payment-s2s-service"] --> DB
architectureFlow.caption: Who depends on whom, and who reads or writes the shared transaction data.

stepByStepFlow: A transaction is created with a unique ORN the moment jop-payment-link-service validates the initiate request.
Each service that touches the transaction (mode service, gateway adapter, completion service) reads or writes against that same ORN.
If a payment combines multiple modes, each mode's contribution is a separate row in the amount table, all linked to the one transaction.
jop-payment-s2s-service is the one service trusted to write the final, authoritative status.

simpleExample.scenario: A customer pays using TWID plus UPI combined for one order.
simpleExample.code:
transactions table:
  ORN: ORD98213 | status: SUCCESS

amount table:
  ORN: ORD98213 | mode: TWID  | amount: 100.00
  ORN: ORD98213 | mode: UPI   | amount: 399.00
simpleExample.input: A single order for 499.00, split across two payment modes.
simpleExample.execution: One transaction row is created under one ORN; two amount rows record how much came from each mode.
simpleExample.output: The transaction resolves to a single SUCCESS status, backed by two amount entries that together add up to the full order value.
simpleExample.explanation: This is exactly why the amount table is separate from the transaction table — a transaction's identity stays singular even when its payment is split across modes.

realWorldExample.scenario: Tracing a single-mode UPI transaction versus a combined-mode transaction through the same schema.
realWorldExample.code:
-- Single mode
transactions: ORN=A1 status=SUCCESS
amount: ORN=A1 mode=UPI amount=499.00

-- Combined mode
transactions: ORN=A2 status=SUCCESS
amount: ORN=A2 mode=TWID amount=100.00
amount: ORN=A2 mode=UPI  amount=399.00
realWorldExample.input: Two transactions — one single-mode, one combined-mode.
realWorldExample.execution: Both use exactly the same two tables; the only difference is how many amount rows exist under the same ORN.
realWorldExample.output: Querying by ORN always returns one transaction row and one or more amount rows, regardless of how the payment was split.
realWorldExample.explanation: The schema doesn't need a special case for combined payments — it's the same structure, just with more rows in one table.

comparisonTables.title: Single-Mode vs Combined-Mode Payment
rows: Still on the one transaction row — combined modes share one status

advantages: Keeping a transaction's identity (ORN + status) on one row, even when split across modes, keeps every other service's query simple.
Documenting data ownership per service prevents two services from writing inconsistent state to the same table.
tradeoffs: A separate amount table means most reads require a join back to the transaction row — simpler writes, slightly more complex reads.
bestPractices: Keep a transaction's identity (ORN + status) on one row, even when its payment is split across multiple modes — model the split in a separate table instead.
Document each service's data ownership explicitly — which table, which columns — so two services never write inconsistent state to the same data.
commonMistakes: Assuming every transaction has exactly one payment mode — combined-mode payments (e.g. TWID + UPI) are a real case the schema has to support.
Letting a service reach into another service's owned table directly instead of going through that service's API.
interviewTips: If asked how you'd model a payment that can be split across multiple methods, this transaction/amount table split is a clean, real answer.
Be ready to name which service owns which piece of data — a common senior-level follow-up after any architecture walkthrough.

faq.q: Why not just add multiple amount columns to the transactions table instead of a separate table?
faq.a: A transaction can combine an arbitrary number of modes — a separate table with one row per mode scales to any combination, where fixed columns would cap how many modes a single transaction could use.

faq.q: What is TWID?
faq.a: It's one of the payment modes JioOnePay supports, which can be combined with others (like UPI) in a single transaction, recorded as separate rows in the amount table under the same ORN.

faq.q: Which service is the source of truth for a transaction's final status?
faq.a: jop-payment-s2s-service — it writes the authoritative outcome to the transactions table, based on the bank's server-to-server callback, not the browser redirect.

summary: Each service documented here has one clear job — from validating a request, to serving checkout, to talking to a specific external provider, to recording the final outcome.

A shared transactions table (one row per ORN, with a status) and a separate amount table (one row per mode contributing to that transaction) is how JioOnePay supports both single-mode and combined-mode payments with the same schema.
Knowing which service owns which piece of this data is what turns a layer-level understanding into a precise, interview-ready one.
quickRevision: Transactions table: one row per transaction per merchant, unique ORN, tracks status.
Amount table: one or more rows per transaction — supports combined-mode payments (e.g. TWID + UPI) as separate rows under the same ORN.
jop-payment-link-service validates + creates the transaction context; jop-payment-s2s-service writes the final authoritative status.
jop-core-pg-details-service is the shared source for merchant config and enabled modes.
Each external provider (Razorpay, PhonePe, Citi, Easebuzz, ...) has its own jop-core-*-service adapter.
jop-pci-ccdc-card-service is the only service allowed to touch raw card data.

Payment Flows — the mode-specific and completion flows in depth

title: Payment Flows
overview: The Payment Lifecycle page showed one complete journey end-to-end; this page zooms into the flows that differ by **payment mode**, plus what happens on **failure and retry**.
Each flow gets its own small, focused diagram rather than one large diagram trying to show everything at once.
Refund and Settlement are intentionally out of scope on this page — they're covered separately.
why: UPI, Wallet, Net Banking, and Card don't all work identically underneath — Wallet and Net Banking specifically have their own routing worth seeing on their own.
Failures and retries are common enough in a payment system that they deserve their own dedicated flow, not just a mention inside a happy-path diagram.
problem: A single diagram trying to show every mode's flow, plus failure handling, plus retries, becomes unreadable — exactly what this page avoids by splitting them.
Without a dedicated Failure Flow, it's easy to assume failures are rare edge cases instead of a normal, designed-for part of the system.

intro: Five focused flows: two payment modes, plus webhook delivery, failure, and retry.

topics.heading: Wallet Flow
points: Wallet is one of the modes handled alongside UPI and Card, selected dynamically per the merchant's configured modes.
It follows the same overall shape as UPI: the Payment Mode Orchestration Layer routes to a wallet-handling service, which proceeds through Gateway Integration to complete the payment.
Wallet can also be one half of a **combined-mode** payment alongside another mode (see Service Details) — recorded as its own row in the amount table.
topics.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["Customer selects Wallet"] --> B["Payment Mode Orchestration"]
    B --> C["Wallet service"]
    C --> D["Gateway Integration"]
    D --> E["Bank / Provider"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Wallet follows the same shape as UPI, just through its own mode service.

topics.heading: Net Banking Flow
points: Net Banking is handled by `jop-ccdcnb-service`, the same service that handles Card payments — mode selection at the orchestration layer determines which path a request takes inside it.
Like Card, this can involve a redirect to the bank's own authentication step before the payment completes.
It converges back to the same Completion Layer (browser redirect + server callback) as every other mode.
topics.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["Customer selects Net Banking"] --> B["jop-ccdcnb-service"]
    B --> C["Bank's own auth step"]
    C --> D["Completion Layer"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Net Banking shares jop-ccdcnb-service with Card, but takes its own path once selected.

topics.heading: Webhook Flow
points: Once `jop-payment-s2s-service` writes the transaction's final outcome, it sends the merchant a webhook containing the result and a checksum.
The merchant's endpoint is expected to acknowledge receipt; if it doesn't, delivery can be retried (see Retry Flow).
This is the merchant's authoritative signal, independent of whatever the customer's browser did via the browser-redirect path.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant S2S as jop-payment-s2s-service
    participant M as Merchant
    S2S->>M: Webhook plus checksum
    M-->>S2S: Acknowledge (2xx)
    Note over S2S,M: If no acknowledgment, delivery is retried
topics.mermaidCaption: The merchant is expected to acknowledge; a missing acknowledgment triggers a retry.

topics.heading: Failure Flow
points: A failure can happen at any layer — a bad checksum at Initiation, an unreachable gateway at Gateway Integration, a timeout waiting on the bank.
Each layer's own failure handling decides what happens locally — reject early, fail over, or mark the transaction ambiguous.
An ambiguous outcome, like a gateway timeout, is deliberately **not** treated as a hard failure — it's marked for follow-up rather than assumed lost.
topics.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Failure occurs"] --> B{"Which layer?"}
    B -->|"Initiation"| C["Reject: bad checksum/config"]
    B -->|"Gateway Integration"| D["Failover or mark ambiguous"]
    B -->|"Bank timeout"| E["Mark PENDING, do not assume failure"]
topics.mermaidCaption: Different layers fail differently — none of them assume the worst by default.

topics.heading: Retry Flow
points: Retries can happen at more than one level: the merchant retrying an initiate request (protected by idempotency via the Redis token), and a service retrying a call to a downstream dependency.
A retried initiate request reusing the same context (the Redis-stored token) is what prevents a duplicate transaction from being created.
This page covers retry conceptually; production-level specifics (exact counts, backoff) are covered in Production & Failure Handling.
topics.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Merchant
    participant PLS as jop-payment-link-service
    participant R as Redis
    Merchant->>PLS: Initiate (attempt 1)
    PLS->>R: Store token
    PLS-->>Merchant: htmlForm
    Note over Merchant: Network issue, no response received
    Merchant->>PLS: Initiate (retry, same request)
    PLS->>R: Token already exists
    PLS-->>Merchant: Same htmlForm (no duplicate created)
topics.mermaidCaption: A retried initiate request reuses the same stored context instead of creating a second transaction.

graph TD
    A["Mode selected"] --> B{"Which flow?"}
    B -->|"Wallet"| C["Wallet Flow"]
    B -->|"Net Banking"| D["Net Banking Flow"]
    C --> E["Webhook Flow"]
    D --> E
    E -->|"Success"| F["Complete"]
    E -->|"Failure"| G["Failure Flow"]

architectureFlow.caption: How the mode-specific flows connect to webhook delivery, failure, and retry.

simpleExample.scenario: A merchant's webhook endpoint is briefly down when JioOnePay tries to deliver the outcome.
simpleExample.code:
1. jop-payment-s2s-service sends webhook
2. Merchant endpoint times out / returns 5xx
3. Delivery is retried after a backoff
4. Merchant endpoint recovers, acknowledges
5. Webhook delivery marked complete
simpleExample.input: A webhook delivery attempt against a temporarily unreachable merchant endpoint.
simpleExample.execution: The failed delivery is retried rather than dropped, until the merchant acknowledges or retries are exhausted.
simpleExample.output: The merchant eventually receives the outcome, despite the temporary outage on their side.
simpleExample.explanation: This is why the webhook is retried, not fire-and-forget — a merchant's own downtime shouldn't mean they never learn a transaction's outcome.

realWorldExample.scenario: A customer's Wallet payment times out waiting on the provider, and the merchant's client retries the initiate call.
realWorldExample.code:
1. Wallet payment times out waiting on the provider
2. Transaction marked ambiguous (PENDING), not failed
3. Merchant's client retries the original initiate request
4. jop-payment-link-service recognizes the existing Redis token
5. Same transaction context is reused, not duplicated
6. Real outcome resolves once the ambiguity clears
realWorldExample.input: A timeout during a Wallet payment, followed by the merchant retrying the original request.
realWorldExample.execution: The timeout marks the transaction ambiguous rather than failed; the merchant's retry reuses the same idempotency context instead of creating a new transaction.
realWorldExample.output: Exactly one transaction exists for this attempt, its real outcome resolved once the ambiguity clears.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is the same safety principle from Architecture Layers applied specifically to Wallet — never assume, never duplicate.

comparisonTables.title: Failure Types by Layer
rows: Circuit Breaker fails over or fails fast

advantages: Splitting flows by concern (per mode, plus failure, plus retry) instead of one all-encompassing diagram keeps each one readable.
Retrying webhook delivery means a merchant's temporary downtime doesn't mean a permanently lost notification.
tradeoffs: Retrying webhook delivery indefinitely risks piling up undelivered notifications if a merchant is down for an extended period — this needs a bounded retry policy, covered in Production & Failure Handling.
bestPractices: Split flows by concern instead of one all-encompassing diagram.
Always retry webhook delivery with backoff — never treat a merchant's temporary downtime as a reason to drop the notification.
commonMistakes: Assuming Wallet and Net Banking work identically to UPI just because they follow the same overall shape.
Treating a retried initiate request as a brand-new transaction instead of resuming the original one.
interviewTips: When walking through a specific mode (Wallet, Net Banking), be explicit about where it's the same as UPI/Card and where it genuinely differs.
A dedicated Failure Flow answer signals maturity — most candidates only describe the happy path unless asked.

faq.q: Does Net Banking always redirect to the bank's own site?
faq.a: It typically involves the bank's own authentication step, similar in spirit to how Card payments can involve the ACS — the exact experience depends on the specific bank.

faq.q: What happens if a webhook is never acknowledged at all?
faq.a: Delivery is retried with backoff; if it keeps failing, this becomes an operational issue to investigate rather than something the system silently gives up on — see Production & Failure Handling.

faq.q: Is a retried initiate request always safe?
faq.a: Yes, as long as it reuses the same request — the stored Redis token means the same logical attempt is recognized and resumed, not duplicated.

summary: Wallet and Net Banking follow the same overall shape as UPI and Card, routed through their own mode-specific services at the orchestration layer.
Webhook delivery is retried, not fire-and-forget — a merchant's temporary downtime shouldn't mean a lost notification.
Failures are handled differently depending on which layer they occur in, and an ambiguous outcome is never assumed to be a failure.
quickRevision: Wallet: same overall shape as UPI, its own mode service, can be one half of a combined-mode payment.
Net Banking: shares jop-ccdcnb-service with Card, typically involves the bank's own auth step.
Webhook delivery is retried with backoff if the merchant doesn't acknowledge.
Failure handling differs by layer: reject early (bad config), fail over (gateway unreachable), mark ambiguous (bank timeout).
A retried initiate request reuses its stored context instead of creating a duplicate transaction.

title: Production & Failure Handling
overview: Building the architecture is one thing — keeping it running correctly under real production load is another.
This page covers how failures are handled across the system, and the broader production concerns any system at this scale has to address.

Some of this is specific to JioOnePay's confirmed stack (Resilience4j, Log4j2, Micrometer/Prometheus, Brave); the rest is presented as general production practice for a system shaped like this one, not asserted as JioOnePay's exact configuration.
why: A payment system failing under load, or failing silently, has a direct cost — a stuck transaction is someone's money in an unclear state.
Production concerns (scaling, deployment, monitoring) are what keep the architecture from the rest of this section actually holding up outside of a design diagram.
problem: Without deliberate failure handling, a slow dependency (a bank, a merchant webhook endpoint) can cascade into a much bigger outage than the original problem.
Without monitoring and alerting, a production issue is only discovered when a merchant or customer complains, not when it actually starts.

intro: Failure handling scenario by scenario, then the broader production concerns around them.

topics.heading: Timeout & Retry
points: A call to an external bank/gateway that doesn't respond in time is treated as **ambiguous**, not failed — already established in Architecture Layers and Payment Flows.
Retries apply backoff rather than retrying immediately, to avoid piling more load onto an already-struggling dependency — a standard production pattern for any external dependency.
Exact retry counts and backoff durations are a tuning decision per dependency, not shown here as a fixed JioOnePay-specific number.

topics.heading: Circuit Breaker (confirmed)
points: Already documented in depth on Architecture Layers, Layer 1 — Resilience4j's Circuit Breaker trips per-route after a failure-rate threshold, redirecting to `/fallback`.
This is JioOnePay's actual, confirmed mechanism — not a generic illustration.

topics.heading: Idempotency & Duplicate Requests
points: A duplicate initiate request is handled via the Redis-stored token from Payment Initiation — the same context is reused instead of a new transaction being created.
This is the concrete mechanism, already established, that prevents a retried request from becoming a duplicate charge.

topics.heading: Callback & Webhook Failure
points: If the merchant's webhook endpoint doesn't acknowledge, delivery is retried — covered on the Payment Flows page.

A callback (server-to-server) failure from the bank's side is different from a webhook failure to the merchant — the former affects whether JioOnePay learns the outcome at all; the latter affects whether the merchant learns it.

topics.heading: Database & Downstream Failure (general practice)
points: A database write failure on the authoritative outcome, via `jop-payment-s2s-service`, is the highest-stakes failure in the system — production systems like this typically use transactional writes and dedicated monitoring on this specific path.
A downstream provider (bank/gateway) outage is handled by the routing and Circuit Breaker mechanisms already covered — this section doesn't introduce new mechanics beyond what's already established.

topics.heading: Observability (confirmed stack)
points: **Logging** — Log4j2.
**Metrics** — Micrometer, scraped by Prometheus.
**Tracing** — Brave.
These are JioOnePay's actual, confirmed tools (from the Edge Layer's tech stack) — used to answer "what's happening right now" across the system, not just at the gateway.

topics.heading: Scaling, Deployment & Rate Limiting (general practice)
points: **Scaling** — stateless services (like the mode and gateway-integration services) scale horizontally behind the Load Balancer, the same pattern covered in System Design's Scalability page.

**Deployment** — a production payment system typically avoids a hard restart in favor of a zero-downtime strategy (rolling, blue-green, or canary) — see CI/CD Pipelines in DevOps for the general mechanics.

**Rate Limiting** — already confirmed at the Edge Layer (Resilience4j Rate Limiter, tunable per route); extending this per-merchant is a natural next step for protecting against one merchant's traffic spike affecting others.

architectureFlow.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["A dependency call fails or times out"] --> B{"Timeout or clear failure?"}
    B -->|"Timeout"| C["Mark ambiguous, do not retry blindly"]
    B -->|"Clear failure, retryable"| D["Retry with backoff"]
    D --> E{"Still failing?"}
    E -->|"Yes, threshold crossed"| F["Circuit Breaker opens"]
    E -->|"Recovered"| G["Continue normally"]
architectureFlow.caption: The general decision path any failing dependency call takes.

stepByStepFlow: A dependency call either times out (ambiguous) or fails clearly (retryable).
Retryable failures are retried with backoff, not immediately.
If failures keep crossing a threshold, the Circuit Breaker opens for that route.
Once the dependency recovers, normal traffic resumes automatically.
Throughout, logs/metrics/traces (Log4j2, Micrometer + Prometheus, Brave) record what actually happened for later investigation.

simpleExample.scenario: A single downstream gateway call times out once, then recovers.
simpleExample.code:
1. Call to Bank X times out
2. Transaction marked PENDING/ambiguous (not failed)
3. Retry (with backoff) succeeds
4. Transaction resolves normally
simpleExample.input: One transient timeout against an otherwise healthy gateway.
simpleExample.execution: The timeout doesn't trigger the Circuit Breaker on its own — a single blip is well within normal tolerance; a retry with backoff resolves it.
simpleExample.output: The customer experiences a slightly longer wait, but the transaction still completes correctly.
simpleExample.explanation: Not every failure needs to escalate to a Circuit Breaker trip — that's reserved for a sustained failure rate, not one blip.

realWorldExample.scenario: A downstream gateway degrades over several minutes, crossing the Circuit Breaker's failure-rate threshold.
realWorldExample.code:
1. Failure rate for Bank X's route crosses the configured threshold
2. Circuit Breaker opens for that route
3. New requests redirect to /fallback instead of waiting on Bank X
4. After the configured wait duration, the breaker allows a trial request through
5. If it succeeds, the breaker closes and normal traffic resumes
realWorldExample.input: A sustained degradation in one gateway's success rate.
realWorldExample.execution: This is the exact, confirmed mechanism from Architecture Layers — the difference here is seeing it in the context of a real production incident timeline, not just the request-level flowchart.
realWorldExample.output: Callers get fast, clean fallback errors during the incident, instead of everyone individually timing out against a known-bad gateway.
realWorldExample.explanation: This is what actually limits the blast radius of a real production incident — without it, one gateway's slow degradation would slow down every request routed to it.

comparisonTables.title: Confirmed JioOnePay Mechanisms vs General Production Practice
rows: Resilience4j, documented in Architecture Layers Layer 1
Documented in Payment Initiation / Payment Flows
Illustrative — not asserted as JioOnePay's exact tuning
Illustrative — follows the same stateless-scaling pattern as System Design's Scalability page

advantages: Distinguishing ambiguous outcomes from confirmed failures avoids over-triggering the Circuit Breaker on normal transient blips.
A confirmed observability stack (logs, metrics, traces) means production issues are visible before a merchant has to report them.
tradeoffs: Retrying with backoff adds latency to the specific request being retried, in exchange for not overwhelming an already-struggling dependency.
bestPractices: Distinguish an ambiguous outcome (timeout) from a confirmed failure — only the latter should count toward a Circuit Breaker's failure rate.
Alert on business-meaningful thresholds (a gateway's success rate dropping), not just infrastructure metrics like CPU.
Keep retries backed off, not immediate, so a struggling dependency doesn't get more load exactly when it can handle the least.
commonMistakes: Treating every timeout as a failure worth counting toward the Circuit Breaker's threshold.
Assuming logging alone is sufficient observability without metrics or tracing to see patterns across many requests.
Deploying via a hard restart instead of a zero-downtime strategy for a system handling live payment traffic.
interviewTips: Be precise about what's confirmed (Circuit Breaker, the observability stack, idempotency) versus general best practice you're reasoning about — this honesty reads as more credible than overclaiming.
If asked about scaling for peak sale events, connect it back to the same stateless-scaling pattern already covered for the Payment API layer.

faq.q: Does JioOnePay use Kubernetes for scaling?
faq.a: Not confirmed here — the underlying pattern (stateless services scaling horizontally behind a load balancer) is the same regardless of the specific orchestration platform used.

faq.q: What's the exact retry count and backoff used in production?
faq.a: Not specified here — these are tuning values configured per dependency, following the same backoff principle already established for Retry in Payment Flows.

faq.q: Is the observability stack (Log4j2, Micrometer/Prometheus, Brave) used everywhere, or just at the gateway?
faq.a: It's documented as the gateway's (Layer 1's) confirmed stack; using the same stack across all services is the natural and typical approach, though this page doesn't assert it's confirmed for every individual service.

summary: Failure handling follows a consistent decision path: distinguish ambiguous (timeout) from confirmed failure, retry with backoff, and let the Circuit Breaker contain a sustained problem.
JioOnePay's Circuit Breaker/Rate Limiter/Bulkhead, its observability stack (Log4j2, Micrometer/Prometheus, Brave), and its Redis-based idempotency are confirmed, real mechanisms.
Exact tuning values (retry counts, scaling platform, deployment strategy) are shown here as general production practice, not asserted as JioOnePay's specific configuration.
quickRevision: Timeout is not failure — mark ambiguous, don't retry blindly (confirmed pattern).
Retries use backoff; sustained failure crosses a threshold and trips the Circuit Breaker (confirmed: Resilience4j).
Idempotency via the Redis-stored token prevents duplicate transactions on retry (confirmed).
Observability: Log4j2 (logging), Micrometer + Prometheus (metrics), Brave (tracing) — all confirmed.
Scaling, deployment strategy, and exact retry/backoff tuning are shown as general practice, not confirmed JioOnePay specifics.
Alert on business-meaningful thresholds, not just infrastructure metrics.
